mappings.rationaleAI: Covers the refraction and reflection principles governing how optical measurement instruments operate (C); directly underpins microscopes (E.1.a), telescopes (E.1.b), and optical measurement devices like polarimeters and refractometers (D.2.e).
E.2 explicitly names cameras as instruments for recording phenomena; the article covers the full range from daguerreotype to digital and scientific imaging, directly addressing this outline path.

Radar is used for direction-finding and ranging (D.2.c), meteorological observation (G.5), and navigation (G.7) — all three are explicitly listed outline paths and are substantively covered in the article's applications section.

E.1.b explicitly names telescopes across the full EM spectrum (optical, radio, infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma-ray); G.6 covers astronomical observations — the article covers both the instrument taxonomy and its astronomical use.

E.1.a explicitly names optical, electron, acoustic, and scanning tunneling microscopes; this article covers exactly those categories (optical, TEM, SEM, fluorescence, scanning probe), directly matching the outline entry.

Shen Kuo made the first recorded description of magnetic declination relevant to magnetic compasses (D.2.c.i), produced raised-relief maps covering cartography (G.2), and led the imperial astronomy bureau with observational contributions (G.6).

B.1 covers systems for measurement of mass; D.1.b names devices for measuring mass and weight including scales and balances — this article defines mass as a measurable quantity and its distinction from weight, directly relevant to both paths.

B.2 addresses standards and techniques for time measurement; D.1.c names mechanical, electric, and atomic clocks — the article covers the full evolutionary sequence from water clocks through atomic clocks, directly matching both paths.

B.2 covers standards for time measurement; D.1.c covers timekeeping devices — the article addresses the SI second, clock types, and time measurement across scales, providing conceptual foundation for both paths.

D.2.c.i explicitly names magnetic compasses and gyrocompasses as direction-finding instruments; G.7 covers navigational techniques — the article covers magnetic, gyroscopic, and GPS-based compass variants and their navigational history.

Lenses are the core optical components determining how microscopes (E.1.a), telescopes (E.1.b), and refractometers (D.2.e) operate; the article covers refraction principles and aberration correction foundational to optical instrument operation (C).

Directly covers outline sections A (theory of measurement), B (units and standards including SI), and C (principles by which instruments operate) — measurement theory, metrology, and the history of standardization are its central topics.

Lasers serve as coherent light sources in interferometers (E.1.d) and other observational instruments (E.1), and as tools in special research apparatus (F); the article covers laser types and applications in measurement, spectroscopy, and lidar.

E.1.a names electron microscopes (TEM and SEM) among observational instruments; F.3 covers particle accelerators — free electrons are the beam in both, and the article explicitly connects electron behavior to both technologies.

D.1.d explicitly names gas, liquid, and electrical resistance thermometers as devices for measuring temperature; the article covers temperature as a scalar property, the Kelvin scale, and thermometer operation — direct coverage of this path.

E.2 covers instruments for recording phenomena (cameras); G.6 addresses astronomical observations, which have relied heavily on photographic recording — the article covers both the instrument basis and scientific imaging applications.

B.1 covers systems of weights and measures (mass and length standards); B.2 covers time standards — SI directly defines and governs all these measurement standards including the metre, kilogram, and second.

D.2.c covers instruments for measuring position by angulation and direction-finding; G.7 explicitly names navigational techniques and devices — the article covers celestial navigation, dead reckoning, inertial navigation, radar, and GNSS.

D.2.f covers instruments measuring ionizing radiation (Geiger counters, scintillation counters); E.1.b covers telescopes across the EM spectrum; G.8 covers radiological techniques — the article spans radio through gamma rays relevant to all three paths.

F.6 names seismographs as special scientific instruments; seismic wave analysis is the primary method for inferring Earth's interior layering, and the article explicitly describes how P and S wave measurements from seismographs inform Earth structure knowledge.
